sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text: [within] Sir John.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: I come, Master Shallow; I come, Master Shallow.
Exit
Enter WARWICK and the Lord CHIEF JUSTICE

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: How now, my Lord Chief Justice, whither away?

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: How now, my Lord Chief Justice, whither away?

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text: How doth the King?

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: Exceeding well. His cares are now all ended.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: Exceeding well. His cares are now all ended.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text: I hope, not dead.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
He’s walked the way of nature, And to our purposes
he lives no more.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
He’s walked the way of nature, And to our purposes
he lives no more.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I would his Majesty had called me with him. The service
that I truly did his life Hath left me open to all
injuries.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I would his Majesty had called me with him. The service
that I truly did his life Hath left me open to all
injuries.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: Indeed, I think the young King loves you not.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I know he doth not, and do arm myself To welcome the
condition of the time, Which cannot look more hideously
upon me Than I have drawn it in my fantasy.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I know he doth not, and do arm myself To welcome the
condition of the time, Which cannot look more hideously
upon me Than I have drawn it in my fantasy.
Enter LANCASTER, CLARENCE, GLOUCESTER, and others

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Here come the heavy issue of dead Harry. O, that the
living Harry had the temper Of he the worst of these
three gentlemen! How many nobles then should hold their
places That must strike sail to spirits of vile sort!

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Here come the heavy issue of dead Harry. O, that the
living Harry had the temper Of he the worst of these
three gentlemen! How many nobles then should hold their
places That must strike sail to spirits of vile sort!

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
Here come the heavy issue of dead Harry. O, that the
living Harry had the temper Of he the worst of these
three gentlemen! How many nobles then should hold their
places That must strike sail to spirits of vile sort!

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text: O God, I fear all will be overturned.

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text: Good morrow, cousin Warwick, good morrow.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER AND CLARENCE
sentences.text: Good morrow, cousin.

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text: We meet like men that had forgot to speak.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text:
We do remember, but our argument Is all too heavy to
admit much talk.

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text: Well, peace be with him that hath made us heavy.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text: Peace be with us, lest we be heavier.

sentences.speaker: GLOUCESTER
GLOUCESTER
GLOUCESTER
LANCASTER
sentences.text:
Though no man be assured what grace to find, You stand
in coldest expectation. I am the sorrier; would ’twere
otherwise.

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text:
Though no man be assured what grace to find, You stand
in coldest expectation. I am the sorrier; would ’twere
otherwise.

sentences.speaker: CLARENCE
sentences.text:
Well, you must now speak Sir John Falstaff fair, Which
swims against your stream of quality.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
Sweet princes, what I did I did in honor, Led by th'
impartial conduct of my soul; And never shall you see
that I will beg A ragged and forestalled remission. If
truth and upright innocency fail me, I’ll to the King my
master that is dead And tell him who hath sent me after
him.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: Here comes the Prince.

sentences.speaker: WARWICK
sentences.text: Here comes the Prince.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text: Good morrow, and God save your Majesty.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE HENRY
sentences.text:
This new and gorgeous garment majesty Sits not so easy
on me as you think. — Brothers, you mix your sadness with
some fear. This is the English, not the Turkish court;
Not Amurath an Amurath succeeds, But Harry Harry. Yet be
sad, good brothers, For, by my faith, it very well
becomes you. Sorrow so royally in you appears That I
will deeply put the fashion on And wear it in my heart.
Why then, be sad. But entertain no more of it, good
brothers, Than a joint burden laid upon us all. For me,
by heaven, I bid you be assured, I’ll be your father and
your brother too. Let me but bear your love, I ’ll bear
your cares. Yet weep that Harry’s dead, and so will I,
But Harry lives that shall convert those tears By number
into hours of happiness.

sentences.speaker: PRINCES
sentences.text: We hope no otherwise from your Majesty.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE HENRY
sentences.text:
You all look strangely on me. [to the CHIEF JUSTICE]
And you most. You are, I think, assured I love you
not.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE HENRY
sentences.text:
You all look strangely on me. [to the CHIEF JUSTICE]
And you most. You are, I think, assured I love you
not.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I am assured, if I be measured rightly, Your Majesty
hath no just cause to hate me.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE HENRY
sentences.text:
No? How might a prince of my great hopes forget So
great indignities you laid upon me? What, rate, rebuke,
and roughly send to prison Th' immediate heir of
England? Was this easy? May this be washed in Lethe and
forgotten?

sentences.speaker: PRINCE HENRY
sentences.text:
No? How might a prince of my great hopes forget So
great indignities you laid upon me? What, rate, rebuke,
and roughly send to prison Th' immediate heir of
England? Was this easy? May this be washed in Lethe and
forgotten?

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I then did use the person of your father; The image of
his power lay then in me. And in th' administration of
his law, Whiles I was busy for the commonwealth, Your
Highness pleasèd to forget my place, The majesty and
power of law and justice, The image of the King whom I
presented, And struck me in my very seat of judgment,
Whereon, as an offender to your father, I gave bold way
to my authority And did commit you. If the deed were
ill, Be you contented, wearing now the garland, To have
a son set your decrees at nought? To pluck down justice
from your awful bench? To trip the course of law and
blunt the sword That guards the peace and safety of your
person? Nay more, to spurn at your most royal image And
mock your workings in a second body? Question your
royal thoughts, make the case yours; Be now the father
and propose a son, Hear your own dignity so much
profaned, See your most dreadful laws so loosely
slighted, Behold yourself so by a son disdained, And
then imagine me taking your part And in your power soft
silencing your son. After this cold considerance,
sentence me, And, as you are a king, speak in your state
What I have done that misbecame my place, My person, or
my liege’s sovereignty.

and propose a son, Hear your own dignity so much
profaned, See your most dreadful laws so loosely
slighted, Behold yourself so by a son disdained, And
then imagine me taking your part And in your power soft
silencing your son. After this cold considerance,
sentence me, And, as you are a king, speak in your state
What I have done that misbecame my place, My person, or
my liege’s sovereignty.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I then did use the person of your father; The image of
his power lay then in me. And in th' administration of
his law, Whiles I was busy for the commonwealth, Your
Highness pleasèd to forget my place, The majesty and
power of law and justice, The image of the King whom I
presented, And struck me in my very seat of judgment,
Whereon, as an offender to your father, I gave bold way
to my authority And did commit you. If the deed were
ill, Be you contented, wearing now the garland, To have
a son set your decrees at nought? To pluck down justice
from your awful bench? To trip the course of law and
blunt the sword That guards the peace and safety of your
person? Nay more, to spurn at your most royal image And
mock your workings in a second body? Question your
royal thoughts, make the case yours; Be now the father
and propose a son, Hear your own dignity so much
profaned, See your most dreadful laws so loosely
slighted, Behold yourself so by a son disdained, And
then imagine me taking your part And in your power soft
silencing your son. After this cold considerance,
sentence me, And, as you are a king, speak in your state
What I have done that misbecame my place, My person, or
my liege’s sovereignty.

sentences.speaker: PRINCE HENRY
sentences.text:
You are right, justice, and you weigh this well.
Therefore still bear the balance and the sword. And I do
wish your honors may increase Till you do live to see a
son of mine Offend you and obey you as I did. So shall I
live to speak my father’s words: “Happy am I that have
a man so bold That dares do justice on my proper son;
And not less happy, having such a son That would deliver
up his greatness so Into the hands of justice.” You did
commit me, For which I do commit into your hand Th'
unstainèd sword that you have used to bear, With this
remembrance: that you use the same With the like bold,
just, and impartial spirit As you have done 'gainst me.
There is my hand. You shall be as a father to my youth,
My voice shall sound as you do prompt mine ear, And I
will stoop and humble my intents To your well-practiced
wise directions. — And, princes all, believe me, I
beseech you: My father is gone wild into his grave, For
in his tomb lie my affections, And with his spirit sadly
I survive To mock the expectation of the world, To
frustrate prophecies, and to raze out Rotten opinion,
who hath writ me down After my seeming. The tide of
blood in me Hath proudly flowed in vanity till now. Now
doth it turn and ebb back to the sea, Where it shall
mingle with the state of floods And flow henceforth in
formal majesty. Now call we our high court of
parliament, And let us choose such limbs of noble
counsel That the great body of our state may go In equal
rank with the best governed nation; That war, or peace,
or both at once, may be As things acquainted and
familiar to us, In which you, father, shall have
foremost hand. Our coronation done, we will accite, As I
before remembered, all our state. And, God consigning
to my good intents, No prince nor peer shall have just
cause to say God shorten Harry’s happy life one day.

And not less happy, having such a son That would deliver
up his greatness so Into the hands of justice.” You did
commit me, For which I do commit into your hand Th'
unstainèd sword that you have used to bear, With this
remembrance: that you use the same With the like bold,
just, and impartial spirit As you have done 'gainst me.
There is my hand. You shall be as a father to my youth,
My voice shall sound as you do prompt mine ear, And I
will stoop and humble my intents To your well-practiced
wise directions. — And, princes all, believe me, I
beseech you: My father is gone wild into his grave, For
in his tomb lie my affections, And with his spirit sadly
I survive To mock the expectation of the world, To
frustrate prophecies, and to raze out Rotten opinion,
who hath writ me down After my seeming. The tide of
blood in me Hath proudly flowed in vanity till now. Now
doth it turn and ebb back to the sea, Where it shall
mingle with the state of floods And flow henceforth in
formal majesty. Now call we our high court of
parliament, And let us choose such limbs of noble
counsel That the great body of our state may go In equal
rank with the best governed nation; That war, or peace,
or both at once, may be As things acquainted and
familiar to us, In which you, father, shall have
foremost hand. Our coronation done, we will accite, As I
before remembered, all our state. And, God consigning
to my good intents, No prince nor peer shall have just
cause to say God shorten Harry’s happy life one day.

Enter FALSTAFF, SHALLOW, SILENCE, DAVY, BARDOLPH, and the PAGE

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Nay, you shall see my orchard, where, in an arbor, we
will eat a last year’s pippin of my own graffing, with a
dish of caraways, and so forth. —Come, cousin Silence.
—And then to bed.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Nay, you shall see my orchard, where, in an arbor, we
will eat a last year’s pippin of my own graffing, with a
dish of caraways, and so forth. —Come, cousin Silence.
—And then to bed.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: Fore God, you have here a goodly dwelling and a rich.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Barren, barren, barren, beggars all, beggars all, Sir
John. Marry, good air. —Spread, Davy, spread, Davy. Well
said, Davy.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Barren, barren, barren, beggars all, beggars all, Sir

said, Davy.

Barren, barren, barren, beggars all, beggars all, Sir

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
This Davy serves you for good uses. He is your
servingman and your husband.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:

servingman and your husband.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
A good varlet, a good varlet, a very good varlet, Sir
John. By the Mass, I have drunk too much sack at supper.
A good varlet. Now sit down, now sit down.—Come,
cousin.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text:
Ah, sirrah, quoth he, we shall Do nothing but eat and
make good cheer, [sings] And praise God for the merry
year, When flesh is cheap and females dear, And lusty
lads roam here and there So merrily, And ever among so
merrily.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text:

year, When flesh is cheap and females dear, And lusty
lads roam here and there So merrily, And ever among so
merrily.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
There’s a merry heart!—Good Master Silence, I’ll give
you a health for that anon.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
There’s a merry heart!—Good Master Silence, I’ll give
you a health for that anon.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text: Give Master Bardolph some wine, Davy.

sentences.speaker: DAVY
sentences.text:
Sweet sir, sit. I’ll be with you anon. Most sweet sir,
sit. Master page, good master page, sit. Proface. What
you want in meat, we’ll have in drink, but you must
bear. The heart’s all.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Be merry, Master Bardolph. —And, my little soldier
there, be merry.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Be merry, Master Bardolph. —And, my little soldier
there, be merry.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text:
[sings] Be merry, be merry, my wife has all, For women
are shrews, both short and tall. 'Tis merry in hall when
beards wag all, And welcome merry Shrovetide. Be merry,
be merry.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE

[sings] Be merry, be merry, my wife has all, For women

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
I did not think Master Silence had been a man of this
mettle.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text: Who, I? I have been merry twice and once ere now.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text: Who, I? I have been merry twice and once ere now.
Enter DAVY

sentences.speaker: DAVY
sentences.text: [To BARDOLPH] There’s a dish of leather-coats for you.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text: Davy!

sentences.speaker: DAVY
sentences.text:
Your Worship, I’ll be with you straight.— [to BARDOLPH]
A cup of wine, sir?

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text:
[sings] A cup of wine that’s brisk and fine, And drink
unto thee, leman mine, And a merry heart lives long-a.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: Well said, Master Silence.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text:
And we shall be merry; now comes in the sweet o' th'
night.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text:
And we shall be merry; now comes in the sweet o' th'
night.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: Health and long life to you, Master Silence.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text:
[sings] Fill the cup, and let it come, I’ll pledge you
a mile to th' bottom.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Honest Bardolph, welcome. If thou wantest anything and
wilt not call, beshrew thy heart. — [to the PAGE]
Welcome, my little tiny thief, and welcome indeed too.
I’ll drink to Master Bardolph, and to all the cabileros
about London.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:

wilt not call, beshrew thy heart. — [to the PAGE]
Welcome, my little tiny thief, and welcome indeed too.

about London.

sentences.speaker: DAVY
sentences.text: I hope to see London once ere I die.

sentences.speaker: BARDOLPH
sentences.text: And I might see you there, Davy!

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
By the Mass, you’ll crack a quart together, ha, will
you not, Master Bardolph?

sentences.speaker: BARDOLPH
sentences.text: Yea, sir, in a pottle-pot.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
By God’s liggens, I thank thee. The knave will stick by
thee, I can assure thee that. He will not out, he. 'Tis
true bred!

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
By God’s liggens, I thank thee. The knave will stick by
thee, I can assure thee that. He will not out, he. 'Tis
true bred!

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
By God’s liggens, I thank thee. The knave will stick by
thee, I can assure thee that. He will not out, he. 'Tis
true bred!

sentences.speaker: BARDOLPH
sentences.text: And I’ll stick by him, sir.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Why, there spoke a king. Lack nothing, be merry. [One
knocks at door.] Look who’s at door there, ho. Who
knocks?

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Why, there spoke a king. Lack nothing, be merry. [One
knocks at door.] Look who’s at door there, ho. Who
knocks?
Exit DAVY

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text:
[sings] Do me right, And dub me knight, Samingo. Is ’t
not so?

sentences.speaker: SILENCE

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: 'Tis so.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text: Is ’t so? Why then, say an old man can do somewhat.

Enter DAVY

sentences.speaker: DAVY
sentences.text:
An ’t please your Worship, there’s one Pistol come from
the court with news.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: From the court? Let him come in.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: From the court? Let him come in.
Enter PISTOL

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: How now, Pistol?

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Sir John, God save you.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: What wind blew you hither, Pistol?

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Not the ill wind which blows no man to good. Sweet
knight, thou art now one of the greatest men in this
realm.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Not the ill wind which blows no man to good. Sweet
knight, thou art now one of the greatest men in this
realm.

sentences.speaker: SILENCE
sentences.text: By 'r Lady, I think he be, but Goodman Puff of Barson.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
Puff? Puff in thy teeth, most recreant coward base!—
Sir John, I am thy Pistol and thy friend, And
helter-skelter have I rode to thee, And tidings do I
bring, and lucky joys, And golden times, and happy news
of price.

sentences.text:
Shall dunghill curs confront the Helicons, And shall
good news be baffled? Then, Pistol, lay thy head in
Furies' lap.

Give me pardon, sir. If, sir, you come with news from
the court, I take it there’s but two ways, either to
utter them, or to conceal them. I am, sir, under the
King in some authority.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
A foutre for thine office!— Sir John, thy tender
lambkin now is king. Harry the Fifth’s the man. I speak
the truth. When Pistol lies, do this [he makes an
obscene gesture] and fig me, like The bragging
Spaniard.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
Come, Pistol, utter more to me, and withal devise
something to do thyself good. Boot, boot, Master
Shallow. I know the young King is sick for me. Let us
take any man’s horses. The laws of England are at my
commandment. Blessed are they that have been my friends,
and woe to my Lord Chief Justice!

the life that late I led?” say they. Why, here it is.
Welcome these pleasant days.

what, thou damned tripe-visaged rascal: an the child I
now go with do miscarry, thou wert better thou hadst
struck thy mother, thou paper-faced villain.

the King do you grace. I will leer upon him as he comes
by, and do but mark the countenance that he will give
me.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
Come here, Pistol, stand behind me. — [to SHALLOW ] O, if
I had had time to have made new liveries, I would have
bestowed the thousand pound I borrowed of you. But ’tis
no matter. This poor show doth better. This doth infer
the zeal I had to see him.

As it were, to ride day and night, and not to
deliberate, not to remember, not to have patience to
shift me—

affairs else in oblivion, as if there were nothing else

every part.

sentences.text: 'Tis so indeed.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
My knight, I will inflame thy noble liver, and make
thee rage. Thy Doll and Helen of thy noble thoughts is
in base durance and contagious prison, Haled thither by
most mechanical and dirty hand. Rouse up revenge from
ebon den with fell Alecto’s snake, for Doll is in.
Pistol speaks nought but truth.

Enter PRINCE HENRY and his train, the Lord CHIEF JUSTICE among them

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: God save thy Grace, King Hal, my royal Hal.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text:
The heavens thee guard and keep, most royal imp of
fame!

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: God save thee, my sweet boy!

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text: My Lord Chief Justice, speak to that vain man.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
[to FALSTAFF] Have you your wits? Know you what ’tis to
speak?

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
[to FALSTAFF] Have you your wits? Know you what ’tis to
speak?

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: My King, my Jove, I speak to thee, my heart!

sentences.speaker: KING
sentences.text:
I know thee not, old man. Fall to thy prayers. How ill
white hairs become a fool and jester. I have long dreamt
of such a kind of man, So surfeit-swelled, so old, and
so profane; But being awaked, I do despise my dream.
Make less thy body hence, and more thy grace; Leave
gormandizing. Know the grave doth gape For thee thrice
wider than for other men. Reply not to me with a
fool-born jest. Presume not that I am the thing I was,
For God doth know—so shall the world perceive— That I
have turned away my former self. So will I those that
kept me company. When thou dost hear I am as I have
been, Approach me, and thou shalt be as thou wast, The
tutor and the feeder of my riots. Till then I banish
thee, on pain of death, As I have done the rest of my
misleaders, Not to come near our person by ten mile. For
competence of life I will allow you, That lack of means
enforce you not to evils. And, as we hear you do reform
yourselves, We will, according to your strengths and
qualities, Give you advancement. [to CHIEF JUSTICE ] Be
it your charge, my lord, To see performed the tenor of
my word.— [To the attendants] Set on.

fool-born jest. Presume not that I am the thing I was,
For God doth know—so shall the world perceive— That I
have turned away my former self. So will I those that
kept me company. When thou dost hear I am as I have
been, Approach me, and thou shalt be as thou wast, The
tutor and the feeder of my riots. Till then I banish
thee, on pain of death, As I have done the rest of my
misleaders, Not to come near our person by ten mile. For
competence of life I will allow you, That lack of means
enforce you not to evils. And, as we hear you do reform
yourselves, We will, according to your strengths and
qualities, Give you advancement. [to CHIEF JUSTICE ] Be
it your charge, my lord, To see performed the tenor of
my word.— [To the attendants] Set on.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: Master Shallow, I owe you a thousand pound.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Yea, marry, Sir John, which I beseech you to let me
have home with me.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
Yea, marry, Sir John, which I beseech you to let me
have home with me.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
That can hardly be, Master Shallow. Do not you grieve
at this. I shall be sent for in private to him. Look
you, he must seem thus to the world. Fear not your
advancements. I will be the man yet that shall make you
great.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:

you, he must seem thus to the world. Fear not your

great.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
I cannot well perceive how, unless you should give me
your doublet and stuff me out with straw. I beseech you,
good Sir John, let me have five hundred of my thousand.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text:
I cannot well perceive how, unless you should give me
your doublet and stuff me out with straw. I beseech you,
good Sir John, let me have five hundred of my thousand.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
Sir, I will be as good as my word. This that you heard
was but a color.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
Sir, I will be as good as my word. This that you heard
was but a color.

sentences.speaker: SHALLOW
sentences.text: A color that I fear you will die in, Sir John.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:
Fear no colors. Go with me to dinner.—Come, Lieutenant
Pistol.—Come, Bardolph.—I shall be sent for soon at
night.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text:

night.

Enter the Lord CHIEF JUSTICE and Prince John of LANCASTER; officers with them

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
Go, carry Sir John Falstaff to the Fleet. Take all his
company along with him.

sentences.speaker: FALSTAFF
sentences.text: My lord, my lord—

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I cannot now speak. I will hear you soon.— Take them
away.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I cannot now speak. I will hear you soon.— Take them
away.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text:
I cannot now speak. I will hear you soon.— Take them
away.

sentences.speaker: PISTOL
sentences.text: Si fortune me tormenta, spero me contenta.
Exeunt all but Prince John of LANCASTER andthe CHIEF JUSTICE

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text:
I like this fair proceeding of the King’s. He hath
intent his wonted followers Shall all be very well
provided for, But all are banished till their
conversations Appear more wise and modest to the world.

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text:
I like this fair proceeding of the King’s. He hath
intent his wonted followers Shall all be very well
provided for, But all are banished till their
conversations Appear more wise and modest to the world.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text: And so they are.

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text: The King hath called his parliament, my lord.

sentences.speaker: CHIEF JUSTICE
sentences.text: He hath.

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text:
I will lay odds that, ere this year expire, We bear our
civil swords and native fire As far as France: I beard
a bird so sing, Whose music, to my thinking, pleased the
King. Come, will you hence?

sentences.speaker: LANCASTER
sentences.text:

civil swords and native fire As far as France: I beard
a bird so sing, Whose music, to my thinking, pleased the
King. Come, will you hence?

sentences.text:

a bird so sing, Whose music, to my thinking, pleased the
King. Come, will you hence?
Exeunt
Enter the EPILOGUE.

sentences.speaker: EPILOGUE
sentences.text:
First my fear; then my curtsy, last my speech. My fear
is your displeasure my curtsy my duty; and my speech, to
beg your pardons. If you look for a good speech now, you
undo me, for what I have to say is of mine own making,
and what indeed I should say will, I doubt, prove mine
own marring. But to the purpose, and so to the venture.
Be it known to you, as it is very well, I was lately here
in the end of a displeasing play to pray your patience
for it and to promise you a better. I meant indeed to pay
you with this, which, if like an ill venture it come
unluckily home, I break, and you, my gentle creditors,
lose. Here I promised you I would be, and here I commit
my body to your mercies. Bate me some, and I will pay you
some, and, as most debtors do, promise you infinitely.
And so I kneel down before you, but, indeed, to pray for
the Queen. If my tongue cannot entreat you to acquit me,
will you command me to use my legs? And yet that were but
light payment, to dance out of your debt. But a good
conscience will make any possible satisfaction, and so
would I. All the gentlewomen here have forgiven me; if
the gentlemen will not, then the gentlemen do not agree
with the gentlewomen, which was never seen before in such
an assembly. One word more, I beseech you: if you be not
too much cloyed with fat meat, our humble author will
continue the story, with Sir John in it, and make you
merry with fair Katherine of France , where, for anything
I know, Falstaff shall die of a sweat, unless already he
be killed with your hard opinions; for Oldcastle died a
martyr, and this is not the man. My tongue is weary; when
my legs are too, I will bid you good night.

First my fear; then my curtsy, last my speech. My fear
is your displeasure my curtsy my duty; and my speech, to
beg your pardons. If you look for a good speech now, you
undo me, for what I have to say is of mine own making,
and what indeed I should say will, I doubt, prove mine
own marring. But to the purpose, and so to the venture.
Be it known to you, as it is very well, I was lately here

for it and to promise you a better. I meant indeed to pay
you with this, which, if like an ill venture it come
unluckily home, I break, and you, my gentle creditors,
lose. Here I promised you I would be, and here I commit

some, and, as most debtors do, promise you infinitely.

the Queen. If my tongue cannot entreat you to acquit me,

Be it known to you, as it is very well, I was lately here
in the end of a displeasing play to pray your patience
for it and to promise you a better. I meant indeed to pay
you with this, which, if like an ill venture it come
unluckily home, I break, and you, my gentle creditors,
lose. Here I promised you I would be, and here I commit
my body to your mercies. Bate me some, and I will pay you
some, and, as most debtors do, promise you infinitely.
And so I kneel down before you, but, indeed, to pray for
the Queen. If my tongue cannot entreat you to acquit me,
will you command me to use my legs? And yet that were but
light payment, to dance out of your debt. But a good
conscience will make any possible satisfaction, and so
would I. All the gentlewomen here have forgiven me; if
the gentlemen will not, then the gentlemen do not agree
with the gentlewomen, which was never seen before in such
an assembly. One word more, I beseech you: if you be not
too much cloyed with fat meat, our humble author will
continue the story, with Sir John in it, and make you
merry with fair Katherine of France , where, for anything
I know, Falstaff shall die of a sweat, unless already he
be killed with your hard opinions; for Oldcastle died a
martyr, and this is not the man. My tongue is weary; when
my legs are too, I will bid you good night.
